items.title: Lauge-Hansen classification
items.contentHtml:
Lauge-Hansen Classification

  • It is based on the position of the foot (supination/pronation) and the direction of force (adduction/abduction/external rotation).
  • Supination-external rotation (most common, ~60%): fibula injury at the level of the syndesmosis (Weber B) or above (Weber C).
  • Pronation-abduction: transverse fracture of the medial malleolus due to avulsion and oblique fracture of the fibula above the syndesmosis.
  • Pronation-external rotation: fracture of the medial malleolus, rupture of syndesmosis and fracture of the proximal fibula (Weber Maisonneuve).
  • Supination-adduction: transverse fracture of the fibula below the syndesmosis (Weber A) and vertical avulsion of the medial malleolus.

items.title: Investment mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Inversion mechanism

  • The inversion ankle sprain (plantar flexion + forced supination) first injures the anterior talofibular ligament (APL).
  • If the force persists, the calcaneofibular ligament (APL) is affected and subsequently the talofibular ligament posterior (LPAP).
  • The inversion mechanism is the most common (85% of ankle sprains).
  • The isolated injury to the LPAA corresponds to a grade I sprain; rupture of the LPAA + LPC to grade II; the complete rupture of the three lateral ligaments to grade III.

items.title: key sign
items.contentHtml:
Key sign in syndesmosis injury

  • The key sign (or piano sign) is explored by compressing the fibula against the tibia at the level of the syndesmosis; if there is pain or abnormal movement, it suggests instability of the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis.
  • It is a classic clinical maneuver to evaluate injury of the syndesmosis (grade II or III in West Point classification).
  • Positivity indicates complete rupture of the anterior tibiofibular and interosseous ligaments.
  • It correlates with Weber C fractures or Maisonneuve lesions.

items.title: Ottawa Rules
items.contentHtml:
Ottawa Classification for requesting x-rays

  • The Ottawa Ankle Rules indicate an ankle x-ray if: pain in the medial or lateral malleolus and at least one of: inability to bear weight (immediate 4 steps or in the emergency room) or pain on bone palpation on the posterior edge of the malleolus (6 cm proximal) or at the base of the fifth metatarsal.
  • For the foot (foot series): pain at base of the fifth metatarsal or navicular + inability to bear weight.
  • Sensitivity >98% for malleolar fractures; avoids unnecessary x-rays in ~30-40% of consultations.

items.foco: Weber injury: classification according to fibula
items.title: Weber classification
items.contentHtml:
Weber injury: classification according to fibula

  • The Weber classification (Danis-Weber) is based on the level of the fracture of the fibula with respect to the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis.
  • Weber A: fracture below the syndesmosis (infrasyndesmal). Stable, conservative treatment except displacement.
  • Weber B: fracture at the level of the syndesmosis (transsyndesmal). Potentially unstable; requires evaluation of the syndesmosis.
  • Weber C: fracture above the syndesmosis (suprasyndesmal). Always unstable; indicates rupture of the syndesmosis and requires open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF).

items.title: Bimalleolar instability
items.contentHtml:
Instability in bimalleolar fracture

  • The bimalleolar fracture (medial malleolus + lateral malleolus) is unstable by definition, since it affects both pillars of the malleolar ring.
  • Displacement of the talus (>2 mm) causes post-traumatic osteoarthritis (up to 50% at 10 years if not anatomically reduced).
  • Requires open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) in most cases.
  • Instability is evaluated with stress radiographs (forced valgus/varus) or CT.

items.foco: Conservative vs surgical treatment in Weber B fracture
items.title: Weber B: conservative vs surgical
items.contentHtml:
Conservative vs surgical treatment in Weber B fracture

  • Weber B fractures can be treated conservatively if they are non-displaced (<2 mm displacement of the peroneal fragment and without instability of the syndesmosis).
  • Surgical indication: displacement >2 mm, instability of the syndesmosis (tibiofibular diastasis >5 mm in mortise view), bimalleolar fracture or injury of the posterior malleolus >25% of the articular surface.
  • Conservative treatment consists of immobilization with a cast or walking boot for 6 weeks, with serial radiographs to rule out late displacement.

items.foco: Anterior drawer test for anterior talofibular ligament
items.title: Previous drawer test
items.contentHtml:
Anterior drawer test for anterior talofibular ligament

  • The anterior drawer test (or Lachman ankle test) evaluates the integrity of the anterior talofibular ligament (APL).
  • Technique: with the ankle in 20° of plantar flexion, the tibia is stabilized with one hand and the heel is moved towards front; anterior movement of the talus >5 mm (or asymmetry >3 mm with respect to the contralateral) is positive.
  • A positive test indicates rupture of the LPAA and suggestive of chronic lateral instability.
  • Sensitivity ~80-90% in acute sprain if performed under local anesthesia.

items.title: Maisonneuve injury
items.contentHtml:
Maisonneuve injury: proximal fibula fracture

  • The Maisonneuve injury is a proximal fracture of the fibula (neck or proximal third) associated with complete rupture of the distal tibiofibular syndesmosis and the deltoid ligament (or fracture of the medial malleolus).
  • Mechanism: pronation-external rotation (Lauge-Hansen).
  • It is a highly unstable injury; Ankle x-ray may show only tibiofibular diastasis, so a x-ray of the entire leg should be included to avoid missing the proximal fracture.
  • Treatment: open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the fibula and fixation of the syndesmosis with a syndesmal screw.

items.title: compartment syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Compartment syndrome in ankle fractures

  • The compartment syndrome in ankle fractures is rare (<2%), but more common in fracture-dislocations of the talus or bimalleolar fractures with great displacement.
  • The most affected compartments are the posterior deep compartment and the lateral compartment of the leg.
  • Clinic: disproportionate pain on examination, pain on passive extension of the fingers, paresthesias and finally absence of pulse (late sign).
  • The diagnosis is confirmed with measurement of intracompartmental pressure (pressure >30 mmHg or differential pressure <30 mmHg with respect to diastolic pressure).
  • Treatment: urgent fasciotomy of the affected compartments.

items.foco: Indication of open reduction in trimalleolar fracture
items.title: Trimalleolar open reduction
items.contentHtml:
Indication for open reduction in trimalleolar fracture

  • The trimalleolar fracture (medial + lateral + posterior malleolus) requires open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) in all cases due to its inherent instability.
  • The posterior fragment (posterior malleolus) must be fixed if it involves >25% of the articular surface on the radiograph lateral ankle, or if there is >1 mm of joint step on the CT scan.
  • Fixation of the posterior malleolus improves joint congruity and stability of the syndesmosis.
  • The approach depends on the fragment: posterolateral route for the posterior malleolus and fibula, and medial route for the medial malleolus.

items.foco: Early rehabilitation in grade I and II sprains
items.title: Early sprain rehabilitation I-II
items.contentHtml:
Early rehabilitation in grade I and II sprains

  • In grade I sprains (stretching of the LPAA without rupture) and grade II (partial rupture of the LPAA + PCL), early rehabilitation accelerates recovery and reduces the risk of chronic instability.
  • Phases: acute phase (0-72 h): protocol PRICE (Protection, Rest, Ice 20 min every 2 hours, Compression, Elevation) + load as tolerated.
  • Subacute phase (3-14 days): active mobilization without load, proprioception exercises (single leg swing) and peroneal strengthening.
  • Functional phase (>14 days): progressive return to walking and sports with semi-rigid orthosis if there is residual instability.
  • Prolonged immobilization (>10 days) is associated with greater stiffness and muscle atrophy.
